text: The patient is a sixty-one year old lady with an history of repeated kidney transplantation, parathyroidectomy for adenoma, who underwent total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingophorectomy with lymphadenectomy in two thousand sixteen. In twenty eighteen she was diagnosed with the rectovaginal fistula. The decision of a multidisciplinary committee was to perform a diverting colostomy to delay repair after patient optimization. After an initial improvement, symptoms worsened. An MRI scan in July twenty nineteen confirmed the rectovaginal fistula.

The patient is positioned in a prone jackknife position with legs open. The robotic system is introduced from the right side, with robotic ports placed along the jet point path. An air seal port is utilized, and three robotic arms are visible. The assistant surgeon is seated between the patient's legs.

The surgeon begins by separating the rectum from the vagina, using diathermy to prepare the rectovaginal septum around the visible blue orifice for subsequent suturing. The dissection is performed in a cephalad direction.
The surgeon uses robotic bipolar forceps to gently lift the rectal wall, developing the dissection plane laterally and carefully dissecting the distal portion of the plane.
The surgeon displays the orifice in the vaginal wall, highlighted in red. Single stitches are applied to close the vaginal wall, completing the suture repair.
The surgeon places a biologic mesh over the vaginal suture to cover the vagina. A V-lock stitch is then used to close the rectum with a running suture, completing the repair process.
and this is the final result. The patient was discharged on postoperative day three after undergoing planned hemodialysis.
